html:
Méliuz (CASH3F/CASH3): A Profitable Loyalty Platform Hidden Inside a Bitcoin Accounting Mess

Méliuz is no longer the broken fintech story most investors remember. The current company is a lean Brazilian loyalty, cashback, affiliate, data and merchant acquisition platform with a Bitcoin treasury attached. That distinction matters because the stock still trades as if the operating business is either melting or irrelevant, while the latest 1T26 release shows the opposite: consolidated net revenue of R$118.2 million, up 18% year over year; Shopping Brasil revenue of R$93.3 million, up 31%; adjusted EBITDA of R$30.1 million, up 74%; and LTM adjusted EBITDA of R$105.3 million on R$478.1 million of revenue.

The stock screen is ugly because reported EBITDA was negative R$46.3 million and net loss was R$60.1 million. That is almost entirely the Bitcoin impairment. Brazilian accounting marks Bitcoin down when market value is below cost, but does not mark it up above cost as profit. In 1T26, Méliuz took a R$76.4 million non-cash hit because quarter-end Bitcoin was below its US$103,322 average purchase cost. The operating business still generated R$16.3 million of adjusted net income in the quarter and R$59.0 million LTM.

That is the mispricing. Investors see a microcap that lost money on Bitcoin. The actual setup is a debt-free company with roughly R$91 million of cash and securities, 604.7 BTC, and a core business currently valued at only about R$135 million to R$155 million depending on quote source. Against R$105 million of LTM adjusted EBITDA, that is roughly 1.3x to 1.5x operating EV/EBITDA. UBS BB made the same basic point, valuing the core at about 1.3x EV/EBITDA and keeping a R$6.20 target before the 1T26 print, according to Money Times.

The old bear case was that Méliuz was a cash-burning cashback app. That is stale. The current bear case has to be different: that the company will waste its operating cash flow on Bitcoin at bad prices, that the core loyalty business will lose consumer trust, or that management will drift back into unfocused product expansion. Those are real risks, but the numbers no longer support the simple “dying app” thesis.

The core business is also less one-dimensional than the market assumes. Shopping Brasil still includes traditional e-commerce cashback, but the real acceleration is in Beyond E-commerce: Méliuz Nota Fiscal, Méliuz Prime, Méliuz Ads, gift cards, mobile recharge, Joy, combined redemption and other monetization layers. In 1T26, Beyond E-commerce revenue was R$20.7 million, up 346% year over year, already 22% of Shopping Brasil. That means the company is gradually moving from “take a cut when someone clicks to a retailer” to “monetize a high-intent consumer base across ads, receipt data, subscriptions, offline purchase behavior and partner campaigns.”

This matters because GMV itself barely grew in 1T26, up only 1% year over year, yet revenue grew 18% and adjusted EBITDA grew 74%. The operating leverage is not just more users buying more stuff. It is better take rate, better mix, lower cashback cost as a percentage of revenue, and fixed cost discipline. The LTM take rate reached 7.6%, up from 7.0%, while cashback cost fell to 43% of net revenue from 45%. Fixed expenses remain below 30% of revenue and marketing is around 11%. With only 231 employees shown on Google Finance and management claiming heavy AI usage in product and operations, the operating model now has enough evidence to deserve a higher multiple than “left-for-dead microcap.”

The retail/social read is messy, but useful. Reddit threads are full of complaints about high-value cashback being delayed or canceled, especially Samsung and AliExpress promotions. That is a reputational risk and probably explains why many Brazilian retail investors refuse to underwrite the consumer brand. But the same threads also show that bargain hunters still route large purchases through Méliuz because the economic value proposition is meaningful. The platform has not lost mindshare; it has a trust problem around high-ticket campaigns. A trust problem caps the multiple. It does not make R$100 million of EBITDA worth R$135 million.

The Bitcoin treasury angle adds both torque and confusion. At the current Bitcoin price around US$78,193 and USD/BRL around 5.07, Méliuz’s 604.7 BTC are worth roughly R$239 million. Add R$91 million of cash and securities, and liquid assets cover about R$330 million of a roughly R$466 million market cap using the current Investing.com quote of R$4.12. The market is therefore assigning only about R$136 million to a business that just crossed R$105 million of LTM adjusted EBITDA.

The apparent “premium to Bitcoin NAV” is the wrong lens unless the operating business is worth zero. If the core is worth even 4x forward EBITDA, the Bitcoin is effectively being valued at a large discount. If the core is worth 5x to 6x, the current stock price is hard to justify even with no heroic Bitcoin assumption.

Capital allocation is the swing factor. Méliuz has already repurchased 7.537 million shares, 82.5% of the authorized program, using about R$30.5 million generated by operations. Management frames this as Bitcoin Yield, because every share retired increases BTC per remaining share. That sounds promotional, but the math is real if the stock is trading below intrinsic value. The best use of cash at this price is not more Bitcoin at US$80,000 to US$100,000. It is retiring CASH3 at 1.3x core EBITDA while liquid assets cover most of the market cap.

The financial services segment is a drag and a known overhang. Banco BV notified Méliuz of its intention to end the commercial agreement, with the notice period running into 2027. Financial services revenue was down 29% LTM and only R$10.5 million in 1T26. That creates a future revenue hole, but it is increasingly not the main value driver. Shopping Brasil generated R$371 million of LTM revenue, up 40%, and Beyond E-commerce is scaling fast enough to offset much of the fintech runoff if execution holds.

The clean base case is not a return to the IPO dream. It is much simpler: 2026 revenue approaches R$500 million to R$520 million, adjusted EBITDA reaches R$115 million to R$125 million, adjusted earnings and free cash flow run near R$60 million to R$70 million, and buybacks continue while the core business is priced absurdly cheaply. Put 5x EBITDA on the operating business, keep Bitcoin at current spot, add cash, subtract a modest haircut for contingencies and corporate friction, and the equity is worth roughly R$850 million, or about R$7.50 per share.

The bull case comes from three things happening together: Beyond E-commerce keeps scaling, the market realizes Méliuz is a profitable loyalty/adtech asset rather than a failed cashback app, and Bitcoin recovers toward or above management’s average cost. At US$110,000 Bitcoin, the BTC portfolio alone would be worth roughly R$335 million. If the core business gets to R$145 million to R$155 million of EBITDA and receives even a 7x multiple, the equity value pushes above R$1.4 billion. That is before assigning much value to future BTC/share accretion through buybacks or any strategic interest in Méliuz’s 52 million registered accounts.

The bear case is not bankruptcy. There is no meaningful debt. The bear case is value leakage: Bitcoin stays below cost, cashback trust weakens, BV runoff is not replaced, fixed expenses creep up, and management keeps talking about strategy instead of harvesting cash. In that scenario, EBITDA can fall toward R$60 million to R$70 million, the market gives it a distressed 2x multiple, cash is partly consumed, and the BTC stack is marked closer to US$50,000. That gets to roughly R$2.80 per share.

At a current stock price of about R$4.12, my targets are:

Bear: R$2.80, down 32%.

Base: R$7.50, up 82%.

Bull: R$12.50, up 203%.

Méliuz is not a high-quality compounder yet. It is a small, messy, ignored Brazilian microcap where the complexity is doing useful work for the buyer: GAAP losses obscure operating profitability, Bitcoin volatility obscures balance sheet value, retail anger obscures continuing platform usage, and the old fintech failure obscures a much leaner loyalty engine. The thesis works if management keeps buying back stock and lets the operating business prove that R$100 million-plus EBITDA is durable.
